findings.summary: Two health_checker.py files coexist in system_discovery/core/ and system_discovery/health/ with incompatible import sets, indicating unresolved module split leaving both alive.
evidence: system_discovery/core/health_checker.py imports numpy, data_visualization.create_line_plot, and coding.execute_code — heavyweight cross-layer deps for a discovery module

system_discovery/health/health_checker.py imports docker, logistics.orchestration.project.workflow_dag, performance, security.digital — an entirely different dependency set from the sibling file with the same name
Both files carry docstrings describing 'health and status checking' with no indication which is canonical
findings.suggestion: Delete one of the two health_checker.py files (the core/ version appears vestigial); move the surviving file to a single, unambiguous location and update all importers.

findings.summary: system_discovery/core/health_checker.py imports data_visualization and coding at module level, pulling heavy optional dependencies into what should be a lightweight foundation module.
evidence: Line 19: 'from codomyrmex.data_visualization import create_line_plot' — visualization in a discovery health-check module is a layer violation
Line 18: 'from codomyrmex.coding import execute_code' — code execution pulled into system-discovery layer at import time
Line 16: 'import numpy as np' — hard dep on numpy in a foundation-layer module
findings.suggestion: Remove data_visualization and coding imports from health_checker; if demo workflows need them, move that logic to a separate demo or reporting file.

findings.summary: Multiple source files place the module docstring after import statements instead of as the first token, violating Python convention and breaking automated docstring extraction.
evidence: events/core/event_schema.py: imports (lines 1-7) appear before the triple-quoted module docstring (lines 11-16)
agents/generic/agent_orchestrator.py: imports on lines 1-10, module docstring 'Multi-agent orchestration utilities.' on line 12
agents/core/base.py: imports on lines 1-7, module docstring 'Core agent interfaces and base classes.' on line 9
findings.suggestion: Move module docstrings to be the very first statement in each file (before all imports); enforce this in ruff or a pre-commit hook.

findings.summary: MCP tool modules follow different error-handling contracts: git_operations wraps exceptions with try/except returning error dicts, but llm/mcp_tools.py has a success path that returns early without a try/except for the Ollama provider.
evidence: git_operations/mcp_tools.py line 36-40: git_is_repo wraps everything in try/except returning {status: error, message: str(exc)}

llm/mcp_tools.py lines 20-38: generate_text has try/except per provider branch, but the function falls through to a bare 'return {status: error ...}' without an outer guard — a missing provider raises no exception, just falls through
calendar_integration/mcp_tools.py raises RuntimeError from _get_provider() — a different error surface than the dict-based error returns used by git/coding tools
findings.suggestion: Establish a single MCP tool error contract: all tools should catch exceptions and return {status: error, message: ...}; no tool should raise to callers. Apply consistently including calendar_integration._get_provider().

findings.summary: The Plotter class in engines/plotter.py is a thin wrapper that only forwards calls to standalone chart functions with no added policy, translation, or state.
evidence: Plotter.bar_chart() body: 'return create_bar_chart(categories, values, **kwargs)' — pure argument forwarding with no added logic
The class stores only figure_size but passes no sizing to the delegated functions, so the stored state is unused
A separate AdvancedPlotter class exists in the same engines/ package, making Plotter a redundant abstraction with one concrete implementation and no callers distinguishing it
findings.suggestion: Either remove the Plotter wrapper and expose the chart functions directly through the package __init__, or give Plotter a real role (e.g., apply stored figure_size, theme, logging) that the functions do not provide.

findings.summary: Core (non-optional) dependencies in pyproject.toml include fastapi, uvicorn, pydantic, pylint, bandit, radon, and numpy — making every install heavyweight even for modules that need none of these.
evidence: pyproject.toml lines 35-60: fastapi, uvicorn, pydantic listed as unconditional [project.dependencies] alongside pylint, bandit, radon, lizard
numpy is a core dependency yet is only imported in visualization and a vestigial health_checker — not truly cross-cutting
Static analysis tools (pylint, bandit, radon, lizard) bundled as runtime deps alongside application deps — typically these belong in dev or optional groups
findings.suggestion: Move fastapi/uvicorn, numpy, and all static-analysis tools (pylint, bandit, radon, lizard) into optional dependency groups; keep core deps to truly universal requirements.

findings.summary: The trust gateway — the critical security enforcement point for all destructive tool calls — has no dedicated test file in the agents/pai/ test directory.
evidence: Glob of src/codomyrmex/tests/unit/agents/ shows: test_integrations.py, test_opencode_integration.py, test_orchestration.py, test_modularity.py, test_orchestration_advanced.py, test_error_handling.py — no test_trust_gateway.py visible
trust_gateway.py is ~820 lines covering TrustRegistry, audit log, confirmation tokens, and security enforcement — a high-value target for test coverage
The agents/pai/ subdirectory of tests contains only README/PAI/SPEC/AGENTS markdown files, no Python test files
findings.suggestion: Create src/codomyrmex/tests/unit/agents/pai/test_trust_gateway.py covering TrustRegistry state machine, SecurityError raising on untrusted calls, confirmation token TTL, and audit log entries.

findings.summary: advanced_plotter.py executes side-effecting calls (plt.style.use, sns.set_palette, warnings.filterwarnings) at module import time, creating invisible global state mutation.
evidence: Line 33: 'plt.style.use("default")' — sets global matplotlib style on every import of this module
Line 34: 'sns.set_palette("husl")' — sets global seaborn palette, overriding any caller preference
Line 35: 'warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=UserWarning)' — globally silences UserWarning for the entire process
findings.suggestion: Move these global configuration calls inside AdvancedPlotter.__init__ or a dedicated configure() method that callers invoke explicitly; never mutate global state at import time.

findings.summary: mcp_tools.generate_chart has a special-cased if/else for 'bar' vs all other chart types, suggesting copy-paste generation rather than a uniform calling convention.
evidence: Lines 43-46: 'if chart_type == "bar": result = factory(data, title=title)' vs 'else: result = factory(**data)' — two incompatible call signatures handled by branching on type name
This suggests the underlying chart functions have inconsistent signatures that were papered over with a conditional rather than normalised
The comment 'Generate a visualization chart and optionally save it' in the docstring merely restates the function name
findings.suggestion: Normalise the chart factory signatures so all accept (data, title=..., **kwargs); remove the bar-specific branch.

findings.summary: The agents/ package root mixes integration adapters, protocol files, and MCP tooling at top level with no grouping, making navigation unpredictable.
evidence: Top-level agents/ contains: education.py, editing_loop.py, mcp_tools.py alongside 15+ provider subdirs (claude/, codex/, jules/, etc.) — mixed concerns at the same level
agents/__init__.py imports from 10+ optional try/except blocks covering ClaudeClient, CodeEditor, AgentInterface etc. — a 50+ line import surface that is effectively a god init
education.py and editing_loop.py are standalone files with no clear ownership group, while provider-specific files live in named subdirs
findings.suggestion: Move education.py and editing_loop.py into appropriate subdirs (e.g., agents/utils/ or agents/workflows/); reduce __init__.py to a minimal re-export surface.

findings.summary: SAFE_TOOLS and SAFE_TOOL_COUNT are exported in __all__ with noqa F822 comments, indicating they are defined via module __getattr__ rather than as normal module-level names — an unusual pattern for simple constant access.
evidence: Line 804: '"SAFE_TOOLS",  # noqa: F822 — defined via module __getattr__'
Line 805: '"SAFE_TOOL_COUNT",  # noqa: F822'
Line 796: comment '# Constants (use the lazy definitions from lines ~198-199)' suggests the constants are generated lazily via __getattr__ rather than defined at module scope
Using __getattr__ for constants that callers import directly is non-obvious and prevents static analysis tools from resolving the names
findings.suggestion: Define SAFE_TOOLS and SAFE_TOOL_COUNT as plain module-level variables (computed once after the tool registry is built); remove the __getattr__ indirection and the noqa suppressions.
